author: stitch.witch
text: Malabrigo Rios is genuinely life-changing yarn. I've been knitting for fifteen years and nothing else comes close for colour depth. Each skein is completely unique and the merino is cloud-soft even after blocking. Yes it's pricey, but you get what you pay for.

author: wool.wanderer
text: Gorgeous hand-dyed colours — no two skeins are alike which makes colourwork tricky but singles projects stunning. The softness is unmatched. My only gripe is dye-lot variation: always buy extra. Shipping to the Netherlands takes a while too.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: For the price, Drops is absolutely unbeatable. I've knitted my entire family in Drops Merino Extra Fine and nobody has complained. It's not the softest yarn on the market but it washes beautifully and the colour range is massive. Their free pattern library is a bonus.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: I'm a Drops convert and not embarrassed about it. Baby Merino is genuinely soft, the price is wild for what you get, and the GOTS certification means I can feel okay about buying in bulk. I wish their supply chain transparency was better but for the price point I can't fault them.

author: fibre.folk
text: Lana Grossa Cool Wool is my go-to for wearable knits. It's a joy to work with — smooth, consistent, and the finished garments look incredibly polished. The eco credentials are a bit thin compared to other premium brands which keeps this at four stars for me.

author: stitch.witch
text: West Yorkshire Spinners is what British wool should be. Knowing the fleece came from British farms, spun in a mill that's been running for over a century — that traceability matters to me as a craftivist. The Signature 4ply is perfect for socks: durable, springy, and not itchy at all.

author: wool.wanderer
text: Solid, honest yarn from a brand that knows what it's doing. The British wool provenance is a real selling point and the GOTS certification backs up the sustainability claims. Slightly rustic compared to fine merino, which suits cables and texture work perfectly.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: Cascade 220 is the workhorse of my stash. Not the flashiest yarn but it does everything: fair isle, cables, colourwork, felting. The fair trade partnerships with Andean cooperatives give me confidence I'm buying responsibly. Washes well and holds its shape for years.

author: fibre.folk
text: Isager is pure Scandinavian restraint. The yarns are designed to layer and combine — you're not buying a single yarn, you're buying into a system. Alpaca 2 and Silk Mohair held together makes the most beautiful halo. Worth every penny for the quality.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: Rowan Felted Tweed is a classic for a reason — the texture, the speckle, the way it blooms after washing. But the price has crept up and I wish the RWS certification was paired with more supply chain transparency. Still a five-star yarn experience, three-star brand ethics.

author: stitch.witch
text: There is no yarn that knits up more beautifully than Rowan in my experience. The patterns, the heritage, the feel — it all comes together. But wow the pricing has become hard to justify. I now treat it as a special occasion yarn and budget accordingly.

author: wool.wanderer
text: Sandnes Garn Sunday is probably my most-used yarn for colourwork sweaters. It's consistent, the colour range is extraordinary, and the GOTS and RWS certifications are the real deal — they actually publish their mill information. Norwegian quality at a fair price.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: Sandnes understands what knitters actually want: reliable yarn, honest certifications, and good colours. Tynn Merinoull is my sock yarn of choice. The triple certification (GOTS, RWS, Oeko-Tex) is the highest standard I've seen from a brand this size.

author: fibre.folk
text: Getting a Wollmeise release is genuinely exciting — the drops happen fast and the community around them is real. The colourways are so complex and saturated that photos don't do them justice. Claudia clearly loves what she does and it shows in every skein.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: I waited three releases before I finally snagged a Wollmeise Twin and it was worth every F5 mash. The colour depth on Bavarian indie dyes is unreal. This is not fast fashion yarn — it's a slow craft experience from a maker who cares deeply about what she produces.

author: stitch.witch
text: Kremke Soul Wool is the gold standard for conscious crafting. GOTS certified, fair trade, plastic-free packaging — they've thought about every stage of the supply chain. The ECO Cashmere is criminally soft. The price reflects the ethics and I'm okay with that.

author: wool.wanderer
text: Everything about Kremke is considered — from the fibres they choose to the way they package the yarn. The Berlin aesthetic suits my style perfectly. Stellaris is a showstopper for shawls. My one wish is a wider colour range in the eco lines.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: Filcolana Peruvian Highland Wool is my most-reached-for yarn for colourwork. The twist is perfect — it splits less than most — and the matte finish makes colourwork patterns pop. Danish minimalism at its finest. GOTS certified which is always a good sign.

author: fibre.folk
text: Filcolana is a reliable, principled brand. I especially appreciate that they publish exactly where their fibres come from. The Tilia laceweight is dreamy for shawls. Not the cheapest option in the Danish range but the quality and ethics justify it.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: BC Garn walks the talk on sustainability — everything is GOTS certified and they've genuinely committed to natural fibres. Hamelton Tweed is perfect for outerwear: sturdy, characterful, and grows more beautiful with wear. A brand I feel proud to support.

author: stitch.witch
text: Noro is a polarising brand and I understand why — the texture can be rough and you'll occasionally find a twig — but the colour gradients are unlike anything else in the yarn world. Silk Garden is a work of art in skein form. I just wish they'd pursue certifications.

author: wool.wanderer
text: I admire the artistry behind Noro's colour gradients but the lack of any certifications is hard to overlook as a conscious crafter. The natural dyeing approach is admirable but with no third-party verification I can't give them full marks. Beautiful yarn, murky ethics.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: Lang Yarns Merino 120 is my entry-level luxury yarn. Perfect tension, consistent thickness, and it wears beautifully over time. Swiss precision is a real thing. I'd like to see stronger eco commitments from them — the Oeko-Tex certification is a start but not enough.

author: fibre.folk
text: Lang Yarns is what I reach for when I need something reliable for a complex pattern. The consistency is excellent — no thins or thicks — which matters a lot for lace. The RWS certification is new but it's a step in the right direction for such a large mill.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: Hedgehog Fibres is the pinnacle of indie dyeing. Bettina's colourways have a painterly quality — depth, movement, and a kind of emotional resonance that mass-produced yarn just doesn't have. Skinny Singles on a BFL base is my desert island yarn. Cork represent!

author: stitch.witch
text: Every Hedgehog skein tells a story. The colourways are evocative — forests, seasons, moods — and the BFL base has just the right amount of sheen. I've gifted Hedgehog to non-knitters and even they understood why it was special. A truly extraordinary indie brand.

author: wool.wanderer
text: Cascade 220 is the yarn I recommend to every beginner because it's affordable, forgiving, and available everywhere. It's not the most exciting yarn but for learning new techniques — short rows, colourwork, cables — it's perfect. Fair trade certification earns real trust.

author: emmi_the_dog
text: Isager Alpaca 2 and Jensen held together is my absolute favourite sweater combination — the warmth-to-weight ratio is incredible and the result looks like something from a Copenhagen concept store. The system approach takes a little learning but the results speak for themselves.

author: stitch.witch
text: BC Garn is the brand I recommend when someone tells me they want to knit more consciously. Triple certified, natural fibres only, plastic-free packaging — it's everything a slow fashion knitter could want. Soft Silk is divine for lightweight shawls.

author: knitwear_rebel
text: Lana Grossa makes genuinely nice yarn but the eco credentials are the weakest of any premium brand I've tried. A single Oeko-Tex certification is table stakes in 2025. The Cool Wool line is excellent but I can't give top marks to a brand that isn't trying harder on sustainability.

author: emmi_the_dog
